speaker: interviewer
text: How did life change following that? Were there changes?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the Russians?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, for-- for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: For-- for me? It was a very good life and a carefree life till the Germans came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: When-- before [INAUDIBLE] the Germans came in, when-- when the war broke out in June, in 1941, in June, so the-- the-- you know, the-- the Russians started to leave our town, you know. They-- they moved from-- they-- they backed off and went back to Russia, you see. So of course they evacuated first their own families, because the Russians came in here, and they settled. I mean, they were [INAUDIBLE] two years. So they settled. So they moved their children, their wives and the children. They moved out back to Russia, even though the border was still there. Even though it was Russia, but the border was still-- our town was on the border. Then--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were invaded by the German army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not yet. Just before, in 1941, in June. Just before we were invaded by the German army, the Russian evacuated you know, into the-- you see, because our town was right on the border. As a matter of fact, after 9 or 10 o'clock you couldn't walk the streets-- on the street, because it's-- it was a border. And then it's no man's land. And then again the Novograd-Volynsk.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what-- what happened, when they-- they moved out first the families, then they said now, look. Now, people, you can run with us. The Germans are coming. Come with us. So many people did go. Packed up and put on the wagon-- horse and wagon, you know, horse and buggy, and all their stuff, whatever they needed. And they did go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And down there, once they come to Zhytomyr, or down to there, the trains, and they took them deeper into Russia. My father packed us all up, and my mother, and my brother, and it was my grandmother. She came to visit us from Russia, but she couldn't-- then June, it broke out this-- the war really between Germany and Russia. And she couldn't go back.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was-- so we were five people. So also on the wagon, we're going to go. Once we came to Zhytomyr, it came a-- that this one-- like propaganda that the Russians are dying of hunger.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's no food. And it's terrible. They're killing the people. So my father said-- and here they started already to bomb. Bombs are falling.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father said it-- the-- the death of the hunger is worse than the death of the sword. We'll go back. Germany can't be that bad. He remembers Germany from Franz Joseph's times, you know. They are nice people, he says. They won't do us no harm. Where-- where in the world were we going down there? To hunger and to die of hunger and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he-- and we returned. We came back into our town. So when we returned, it was-- it turned to hell. The Germans already invaded us, you know, because [INAUDIBLE] we came. There was no food. And when they Germans right away, they-- they established a militia, a Ukrainian militia. And they were dressed in blue and yellow, light blue. Till now I can't look on those two colors, the blue and yellow. When I look at the colors, I just shiver. And they were-- they were so-- you know, they were throwing around their weight. And they-- right away they said, on every Jewish home has to be a Magen David, on every Jewish home. And everyone has to wear a white armband with a Magen David on it. The blue one, embroidered. And it has to be nice and clean and white. It can't be dirty. Every night we washed it, and we starched it. We didn't have starch, so we-- we took flour, and so on. And-- and ironed it. And we wore the-- the-- the

speaker: interviewee
text: The Star of David.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Star of David, you know. Then it came they were going to take us to work. They had to report every day. Everybody had to report to the Judenrat. They established a Judenrat, you know. And they had to-- we had to report to the Judenrat. If we went to work or not, but we had to come and report like, hello, here I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know any-- did you know anybody from the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Judenrats-- they-- they established the people between themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah, I was a child--

speaker: interviewer
text: Personally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there was [? Broder. ?] He was the head of the Judenrat. There was [? Krasenstufski, ?] which his son is in Israel. I went with him to school together, you know. So he was the head of Judenrat at one time. And-- and then they came out that we don't have to wear the armband, only the young children. They wear a-- a-- a yellow-- yellow patch, one in the front, and one in the back. But of course we still could go and work by the-- by the-- then the non-Jews in the field or something to earn something to eat, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we used to trade a lot, a blouse, a skirt, a pair socks with-- for food. I mean, but how much do you have already to trade, you know? So one time I went with my mother. And years ago they were wearing shawls. So she was wearing a shawl, and she covered up that-- the-- the-- the yellow patch. Came over police. Militia came over. And he came over right to him, to us. And she says, why do you cover up? And my mother nodded, so she-- he went and beat her up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And beat me over there. And we were lucky he didn't kill us. And they let us go. So we went. We-- we ran home, the other way.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, how can you feel when-- when they beat up your mother? So then, those days-- I don't know how it's now-- children would give their life for their parents, you know? Parent, of course, twice as much. I-- I said, from now on, I go by myself. And I used to run. You know, I was short and dark. Many times they figure out I'm a [INAUDIBLE] used to go and dig potatoes. I'd work the day, and I'd get a [NON-ENGLISH] potatoes. [NON-ENGLISH] is 60 pounds. And carry on my back, and bring it home. But bread was given out 100 gram per person. Since we were four people we could get-- you know, my grandmother was still alive. Because my father wasn't. That's another story about my father. So they gave us-- so I used to go and get a 400 gram of bread. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get the bread?

speaker: interviewee
text: The bread-- there was a-- a store which gave out the bread. So used to get up 3: 00 in the morning. Used to stay in line. And my mother put books in front-- in the front and in the back of me, books, and put my brother's coat, jacket, the big one, and tied me with a rope so when they would hit me, the Germans, so I won't get hurt.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was-- I went out one time, was 3:00 in the morning,

speaker: interviewee
text: 00. It was very cold. Was already towards the fall. And the-- as I was staying in the line for the bread, just came my turn. He wouldn't let me in. And they took me away, [INAUDIBLE]. [? Job. ?] They took us to do some cleaning at the synagogue. There were the horses. We had to clean up all the dirt. You know, where the horse had--

speaker: interviewee
text: They kept the horses in the synagogue.

speaker: interviewee
text: They kept the horses in the synagogue. The synagogue we were going, [INAUDIBLE] Synagogue. So I didn't-- I didn't mind they already went to work. But I didn't-- I was worried my-- my mother wouldn't know where I was. But anyway, it was turning dark when they let us go home, so when I came home, she was so happy to see me that-- and then, on top of that, I returned with all the bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have tickets to get the bread? How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. They didn't give. You just-- they-- they-- they took-- they just, you know, we didn't have ticket--

speaker: interviewee
text: You just line up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. We just lined up. But they asked-- you wouldn't dare lie. You know what I mean? If [INAUDIBLE] for how many people? For four people. You know, you don't dare lie. But many times they had themselves fun. We'll stay in a line and a big truck will come in, a German truck. And then a few will get killed. They just run straight into the line, because was a-- but with my father, you see, this was later. But when my father-- when they came into town, that was in-- that was-- it's in September. It's a Ukrainian [NON-ENGLISH], it's called. It's a holiday. They decided to get [? themself ?] happy and go lucky, the police, the-- the-- the-- the Ukrainian militia, and they decided they're going to get some men to work. They-- they-- I was going with my father when a guy, [? Redke, ?] he just died. He was there-- grabbed my father and he says, we're taking you to work. You'll be in the evening. You'll come home. So my father says, go home and tell mother that I'll be in the evening home. Something a child feels in-- in my heart, I felt that's the last I'll see of my father. He kissed me and he says go, sweetheart. He didn't say sweetheart. [NON-ENGLISH], go home, and tell your mother. Tell mother not to worry, I'll be home soon. I'll be home in the evening. All the truth is coming out now from the Ukrainians what happened. They took the people to work. And we knew they're taking [NON-ENGLISH]. That's a place seven kilometer from our town.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were building down there the road. As I was-- was now, they said, see this road? Your father built. They-- what they told us, they were afraid because they took the able people, they shouldn't rebel or something, and they took them to build that road. And as they got weak, they killed them off slowly.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I-- when they took our father and he didn't come, so people used to come that they saw in [NON-ENGLISH]. They said [NON-ENGLISH] is there, and [NON-ENGLISH] is there. And they-- they named the names. So we know they-- they were there. And says that's all they want is food, bread. And you know, we didn't have no food. So we used to go and get grain and then the-- it was called [NON-ENGLISH], you know, on a mill between two stones. We ground it like this. And baked up-- they took advantage on us, and they took away the food [INAUDIBLE]. And we baked, and just to send something so they will have. To make a long story short, we never saw them. We never saw them again. We knew they were- killed them right behind the-- the-- the-- the sugar factory. We had a sugar factory to make sugar. And it's in [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how do you know--

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: --that they were killed there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- the-- the Ukrainians came and told us. You know, the people. There-- there-- there were-- you know, right after the war. But we weren't able to go there. The Russians didn't allow to go, because it was still belong-- that part belonged to Russia when the Russians came and liberated later. They-- we weren't allowed to go there. So that was that. So my [INAUDIBLE] was actually my brother, mother, myself, and grandmother. And your grandmother took sick with a heart attack and she died. And we were afraid to-- to-- to-- to go to the-- to the cemetery you know, to-- to-- to make the funeral,

speaker: interviewer
text: To bury her?

speaker: interviewee
text: To bury her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were you afraid?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we were afraid to go out on the streets, you know. But, I mean, you have to. So, but surprisingly, the Germans stood aside, and she says [INAUDIBLE], and they give respect, surprisingly, you know. So we buried. But then it started a very hard life. Twice as hard. They told us that every one, every other day that came down, that they-- they need curtains. They need silver. They need gold. They need money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is they?

speaker: interviewee
text: They need-- the Germans. So they came from the Judenrat and they say today we want so much silver. So whatever we had, we gather from house to house, whatever we had in the house. If we had a goblet, or we have spoons, cutlery, anything, we gathered. And we gave. We didn't understand that time, but it's almost like buying a day of life. You know, now, when I look at it, I knew. I know. But that how it-- it was that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the Judenrat treat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Judenrat were wonderful. I mean, they-- they-- the German, you know, the burgomeister, whatever, came. I don't know if it's burgomeister. I know from the army, came to them, and they approached us, you see. There was a man, [NON-ENGLISH], and he couldn't take it no more. He committed suicide. He couldn't-- he felt that he is selling out the-- the-- the-- the-- the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was one of the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the head of the-- it, you know, and then [? Broder ?] came up. There was no rumors. There's nothing. We didn't hear a thing about any killing, about what was going on. We didn't know what was going on at the next town.

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1942, because my father was killed in '41. We had a very severe winter. We didn't have woods to heat the-- the-- the-- the-- the-- our-- our houses were heated with-- with wood, you know. I mean, so primitive and so on. And when-- when-- when it-- when it came the first year, you know, it-- it was-- it was hard to-- to explain. You weren't a person. And somehow, I don't know why I was a child, but I constantly kept crying. I don't know why. But I says, I want my father. I want my father. I don't know why. You know, I mean, I was already a big girl. I should have known better, but somehow-- then, one morning, it was-- it was-- spring was coming, you know, [NON-ENGLISH]. We-- we-- we were going to-- were afraid to bake [NON-ENGLISH]. We didn't. But the rabbi said that we can have from buckwheat. If we make rolls from buckwheat, that will be kosher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just ask you, at that time, were you already closed in a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not yet. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not yet. So we-- the buckwheat. So we-- we baked. And-- and during the night, you know, baked. And so we had-- that was supposed to be the [NON-ENGLISH]. And to tell you the honest truth, I used to go and steal--

speaker: interviewer
text: From--

speaker: interviewee
text: --in the gardens, from the-- the-- the-- at nighttime.

speaker: interviewer
text: From whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: From-- if it was the non-Jewish people. [INAUDIBLE] gardens. We had a lady which had milk, used to bring us milk. Another one, you know, fruit, you know, from the gardens. We used to buy, bring them to the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I used to go and steal in the gardens. Onions, raw onions, I mean-- I mean, you do anything for food. And then they-- they used to bring to the sugar-- beets for the sugar factories, and bring down the wagons whenever they fell under, as they were driving, to fall down. We used to got and pick them up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some days we were hit by the goy. Used to hit us. We're not even supposed to pick it up off the ground. And then we used to grate it and dry it out. And that was like sugar, and we drink it with water. I mean, it was some nourishment, you know. It was-- it was a very, very hard life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then it came one morning, my brother worked for the Germans. How did he work? He worked for [NON-ENGLISH], for the-- it had to do with the-- with-- with the roads, you know, with-- so on, you know. And one-- a German liked him. And he just-- he always gave some food and so on. So in the evening, he came home. And he was working for them. And like I went to work too. But we-- we had to work for cleaning and those things.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he worked for them in the office, you know. He was very-- he was very knowledgeable, also, about the forestry. So I guess they knew-- they-- they appreciated him. Who knows. But I mean, they were very good to him. As a matter of fact, one saved his life. The-- a German, you know, from-- an officer saved my brother's life. But anyway, when we came to-- to-- one morning, we got up. I didn't get up. My brother came that evening home, and he wasn't feeling well, was sick. My mother wakes us up, and she said, you know what, there's [NON-ENGLISH], you know, each one had a name. And there beating him so badly up, I said, and there's so many people on the street. And they're gathering so many people. And I don't know what's-- what's on. He says dress up. Usually when we-- when we went to sleep we always went with our clothes, in case we had to run or something with our clothes. So we put another dress on top of another one, you know. And we came and we went out. As I was going--

speaker: interviewer
text: To the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we went out into-- on the-- on the-- on the side door, and to go in the back of somebody else's house, like to hide. We didn't know what was going on. And as we were coming, hiding in the backyard of somebody's, a man came. I-- one of the-- the policemen, Ukrainian, came and grabbed us. And they grabbed us and they brought us into a place where it's-- by that time, you know, we were walking the streets like in a rope. Grabbed us and put us in-- in the line. People were walking. And we were walking there. And there was a lady. This I'll never forget. Her name was [? Bisou. ?] She-- she went out of the lines, so he went and shot her. And she was laying with her hands in the-- you know, and her keys were straight in her hands, yeah, as if this-- she locked the door. But you know, the children were around. They-- they watched us, the Ukrainian and the Polish people. They all watched us and the going.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know what? Deep in my heart, I envied them so much. I envied them so much. You have no idea. I says, why can't I be in her place? Why can't I be [? Sasha, ?] you know, the-- the next door neighbor. I wanted so badly be her. Isn't it-- that how much I envied her at that-- at that moment. Did she said anything to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nobody said nothing to us. No one said nothing, a word, to us. But I was so envious. Then they-- we came to a place on the street. It's [NON-ENGLISH], the street. And there's [? Feldman's ?] house, a big house. At that time, when we came, mountains of clothes. My mother wore a coat. She had to take off the coat. She had to take off her rings, you know. So she took off, and she gave it. And we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Gave it to whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the-- to the men. To-- and the clothes. You had to take off the coat and put it on a mountain. Mountains, already, of clothes were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was supervising that?

speaker: interviewee
text: A German, you know. Was [NON-ENGLISH]. A Nazi with-- with-- with the-- with the-- and we walked out. And then told us to go back. Anyway, there were so many feet deep, honest to God, on the street, you know, with people. I was staying down there with-- with a little girl. She went to school together from our street. [? Areva ?] [? Smaller. ?] Her brother, two brothers, still live there in Toronto. And she said to me, she turns around to her mother, and she says, mom, I don't-- I can't see you being dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said to me, you know what? No, I turned around to her, I said, can't neither. I said, let's stay in front of our mothers. So when they kill, maybe they'll kill us first, or if they kill them, we won't see.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know where they're taking us. Someplace to kill or something. We know they're going to kill us, but you know-- by that time, they came and took us away again over the road, two streets further on a marketplace. When we came to that marketplace I said to-- I was staying with-- with my mother, you know, and I was so hugging her.

speaker: interviewee
text: A German came over to me and he said, how old are you? And I was going to say 14, and she's-- my mother pushed me. She's 16. Just like that. In what language did he ask you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in German, you know. So I said, she's 16, and she pushed me. And as she pushed me, I pushed myself back. And I was hanging on to my mother. Apparently, he wanted to spite me, so he grabbed me so hard by the hand, he just gave me like a throw. Come. And took. And he took me. That was the last I saw my mother. And they took us in a place where we're sitting-- women were sitting, and then on the other side, I give a look. My brother is between the-- the men.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in the meantime, we hear noises. Like, you could hear shooting, bang, bang, bang, bang, bang. You know I mean? You could hear it all. And in front, by this side, as we were sitting on-- on one-- this one-- Benches?

speaker: interviewee
text: No benches. On the ground we were sitting. Benches, that would have been a luxury. They sat us on the ground, but in a-- in a yard. Somebody else's-- [? Klener's ?] yard. [? Klener's ?] yard. Then as-- and in the back, we turned around. In our town we had [? Razel. ?] Three-- two sisters and a mother. A whole year they were fine. It came summertime, spring, they-- they were mentally.

speaker: interviewee
text: These [? Razel ?] had a baby, a-- a-- you know. So they made her-- they made her dance. And they made her sing. And she was singing heil Hitler, live Hitler. You know what I mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: They made her. And they grabbed that child from her, and down there was a fence. And they took the child and they smashed it against the fence.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was sitting there. And then, we were all stunned. You know, there's no tears. There's-- and-- you know, you-- you just go to-- you-- you-- there's only a will to survive somehow. I don't know. There's-- you have no feelings anymore. You're not a human. I can't believe it, that I could see all that. Then she-- then they told us-- they come out, and they said, you people are left in here to work. And you've got to keep us-- our places clean, and so on. And they left that time seventy women and some men, so we would do all the cleaning for them, you know. And-- but, and tomorrow we have to report to this and this place, which was also [NON-ENGLISH] Street.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went home. Where would I go? People which knew what was going on, which they heard already, it was late. They went in the attics and pulled up the ladders, you know. It was a mass killing till about 4:00. 4:00, it stopped. They-- they were-- the old people that couldn't walk, either they shot them on the road or they put them on wagons, on the wagons, and they were going over there. Wagons, small children. 04:00 finished the mass killing. You know what I mean? Go home. And tomorrow report to there. I went home. What home? Was nothing. They robbed everything. Everything was taken out of the house. All our clothing, the furniture, everything. They went-- where just one lady saved me a sweater, which was Pascha.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had no place to go. I came to next door, and I said, Pania Anitska, I don't have a mother. I've nobody. She turns around-- our curtains were hanging there already in her house. She says, get out from here, you dirty Jew. Now it's my time. Your time is over. I says Pania Anitska, I mean, I grew up in her home. When it was Christmas, she always said something for me on the tree, you know, because those days they gave out candies or books or something. She always had something for me. When my mother was-- was cross with me, I used to run to Pania Antiska if I cut myself [INAUDIBLE]. And here she-- [SOBBING] stayed over home. We came-- we came the next day. We came to-- to the place, to where they told us to come. He says, from now on, you all have to gather into a ghetto. And they put us into a ghetto. So many people in one room, was no place where to turn around.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your house--

speaker: interviewee
text: No food.

speaker: interviewer
text: --part of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had to go to another place.

speaker: interviewee
text: We have to go into another part of the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you stay with?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were some people. I didn't even know them. You know, some from a village with some, so I stayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was-- was-- was coming too, but he was out of the town, you know. He was working that time. And I didn't know he was all the time working underground for the-- for the Russians, for the partisan. All along he was working for the underground. But once in a while he was able to come to the-- I mean, when he came to the ghetto, he was able to bring honey, some bread. But they allowed us for a few hours to get out of the ghetto to go to market. Two hours a day.

speaker: interviewee
text: But with what are you going to buy the stuff? There's nothing. I remember I cut off buttons and I traded I think from my coat four buttons, five for some food.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I came down to this Pascha. And I came over to her and I said her, Pascha, I don't have nobody. She said to me, I've got something for you. You see, in school we took hand work, you know. Knitting. She knew I was knit-- she used to deliver milk to us. She knew I was knitting a sweater. She says, everybody was robbing. And everybody was taking things away. So I made a little parcel. I figure out maybe sometimes you would come. I saved you this, you'd have. She gave me that little parcel. And she said to me, I've got some-- here's some milk and here's some bread. She gave me bread to take. She says, but I'm telling, I'm afraid you should come here. Because, I mean, her life was also in jeopardy. But if you can sneak out once in a while, I'll give you bread and milk.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she said to me, but one thing I must tell you, you was-- tell you about-- your mother came to my dream. And she said to me she is very happy where she is. The only thing, it's wet for her, because you cry a lot. So I don't know if she wanted to make me feel that way or if it's true. She said, don't cry, because you're hurting your mother. I said, Pascha, I won't cry no more. And you know what? I never cried.

speaker: interviewee
text: I never, I never cried. I was as hard as a rock. And I just says, I have to survive. I have to survive.
